questions.text: Pearl is mainly constituted of
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Calcium Carbonate**

Pearls are mainly composed of **calcium carbonate (CaCO3)** arranged in crystalline layers.

Option Analysis:
• Calcium Oxalate → Found in some kidney stones.
• Calcium Sulphate → Found in gypsum and plaster of Paris.
• **Calcium Carbonate → Correct. Major component of pearls and shells.**
• Calcium Oxide → Quicklime.

questions.text: What are Rubies and Sapphires chemically known as?
questions.solution:
**Question**

**What are Rubies and Sapphires chemically known as?**

A. Silicon Dioxide
B. Aluminium Oxide
C. Lead Tetroxide
D. Boron Nitride

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Aluminium Oxide**

---

**Core Concept**

**Ruby and Sapphire** are varieties of the mineral **Corundum**.

The **chemical composition of corundum** is:

Al₂O₃

This compound is **Aluminium Oxide**.

Both gemstones have the **same chemical composition**, but their **colors differ because of impurities**.

---

**Why Ruby and Sapphire Have Different Colors**

Even though both are **Al₂O₃**, small **impurities change the color**.

**Gemstone — Impurity — Color**

Ruby — **Chromium (Cr)** — **Red**

Sapphire — **Iron / Titanium** — **Blue**

So chemically they are **Aluminium Oxide**, but **trace elements produce different colors**.

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)**

Silicon dioxide is the **chemical composition of:**

• **Quartz**
• **Sand**
• **Amethyst**
• **Agate**

It is **not the composition of ruby or sapphire**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**B. Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃) ✅**

Ruby and sapphire are both **forms of corundum**, which is **Aluminium Oxide**.

**Properties of corundum:**

• **Very hard mineral**

• **Hardness = 9 on Mohs scale**

• **Second hardest natural substance after diamond**

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

**C. Lead Tetroxide (Pb₃O₄)**

Lead tetroxide is also called **Red Lead**.

**Uses:**

• **Paint pigment**

• **Rust-proofing coatings**

It has **no relation to ruby or sapphire**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**D. Boron Nitride (BN)**

Boron nitride is a **synthetic industrial material**.

**Uses:**

• **Lubricants**

• **High-temperature materials**

• **Cutting tools**

Although **cubic boron nitride is very hard**, it is **not related to ruby or sapphire**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Important Exam Fact**

**Gemstone — Chemical Composition**

Diamond — **Carbon (C)**

Ruby — **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)**

Sapphire — **Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)**

Quartz — **Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂)**

---

**Typical Exam Question**

**Ruby and Sapphire belong to which mineral group?**

➡ **Answer: Corundum**

---

✔ **Final Answer: Aluminium Oxide (Al₂O₃)**

questions.text: These days yellow lamps are frequently used as street light. Which one of the following is used in these lamps?
questions.solution:
**Question**

**These days yellow lamps are frequently used as street lights. Which one of the following is used in these lamps?**

A. Sodium
B. Neon
C. Hydrogen
D. Nitrogen

✅ **Correct Answer: A. Sodium**

---

**Core Concept**

The **yellow street lights** commonly seen on roads are called **Sodium Vapour Lamps**.

These lamps contain **sodium metal vapor**, which emits **bright yellow light** when **electric current passes through it**.

The yellow color occurs because **excited sodium atoms emit light mainly around 589 nm wavelength**, which appears **yellow to the human eye**.

---

**Why Sodium Lamps Are Used for Street Lighting**

Sodium vapour lamps are used because they:

• Produce **very bright yellow light**

• Have **high luminous efficiency**

• **Consume less electricity**

• **Last longer**

This makes them **ideal for highways and street lighting**.

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Sodium ✅**

In a **sodium vapour lamp**, sodium metal is **heated and vaporized**.

When **electric current passes through the sodium vapor**:

• Sodium atoms become **excited**

• They release energy as **yellow light**

This is why **street lights appear yellow/orange**.

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

**B. Neon**

Neon gas produces a **reddish-orange glow**.

It is mainly used in:

• **Neon sign boards**

• **Advertising lights**

It **does not produce the yellow light used in street lamps**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**C. Hydrogen**

Hydrogen gas is:

• **Highly flammable**

• Used in **rockets and chemical processes**

It is **not used in street lighting lamps**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**D. Nitrogen**

Nitrogen is an **inert gas**.

Uses include:

• **Fertilizer production**

• **Filling bulbs to prevent oxidation**

But it **does not produce the yellow street light glow**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Important Exam Fact**

**Lamp Type — Gas Used — Color Produced**

Sodium vapour lamp — **Sodium** — **Yellow**

Neon lamp — **Neon** — **Red / Orange**

Mercury vapour lamp — **Mercury** — **Bluish white**

---

**Typical Exam Question**

**Which lamp produces yellow light used in street lighting?**

➡ **Answer: Sodium vapour lamp**

---

✔ **Final Answer: Sodium**

questions.text: Sodium Vapour Lamp is usually used as street light, because
options: Light from this is monochromatic and will not split through water droplets
It is pleasing to the eyes
questions.solution:
**Question**

**Sodium Vapour Lamp is usually used as street light, because**

A. These are cheap
B. Light from this is monochromatic and will not split through water droplets
C. It is pleasing to the eyes
D. It is brightly illuminating

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Light from this is monochromatic and will not split through water droplets**

---

**Core Concept**

A **sodium vapour lamp** emits almost **monochromatic yellow light** with wavelength around **589 nm**.

**Monochromatic light means:**

Light of **only one wavelength / one color**.

Because of this property, the light **does not disperse (split into different colors)** when passing through **water droplets or fog**.

This is why **sodium lamps are very effective for street lighting in foggy or rainy conditions**.

---

**Why This Is Important for Street Lights**

In **rainy or foggy weather**:

• **White light splits into many colors** due to dispersion.

• This **reduces visibility**.

But **monochromatic sodium light**:

• **Does not split easily**

• **Penetrates fog better**

• Provides **clearer visibility on roads**

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. These are cheap**

Cost is **not the main scientific reason** for using sodium lamps.

Although they may be economical, the main reason is their **optical property (monochromatic light)**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**B. Light from this is monochromatic and will not split through water droplets** ✅

Sodium lamps emit **nearly single-wavelength yellow light**.

Because it is **monochromatic**:

• It **does not undergo dispersion easily**

• It passes through **fog, mist, and rain droplets more effectively**

This **improves visibility for drivers at night**.

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

**C. It is pleasing to the eyes**

The **yellow light is not considered visually pleasant** compared to white light.

Modern **LED lamps** are preferred where **visual comfort** is needed.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**D. It is brightly illuminating**

Sodium lamps are **efficient**, but **brightness alone is not the main reason**.

The **key property used in exams is monochromatic light and reduced dispersion**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Important Exam Fact**

**Lamp — Type of Light**

Sodium vapour lamp — **Yellow, monochromatic**

Mercury vapour lamp — **Bluish white**

Neon lamp — **Red-orange**

---

**Key Term (Very Important)**

**Monochromatic light**
= **Light consisting of a single wavelength**.

**Example sources:**

• **Sodium vapour lamp**

• **Laser**

---

✔ **Final Answer:**

**B. Light from this is monochromatic and will not split through water droplets**

questions.text: The most commonly used substance in fluorescent tubes are
questions.solution:
**Question**

**The most commonly used substances in fluorescent tubes are**

A. Sodium Oxide and Argon
B. Sodium Vapour and Neon
C. Mercury Vapour and Argon
D. Mercuric Oxide and Neon

✅ **Correct Answer: C. Mercury Vapour and Argon**

---

**Core Concept**

A **fluorescent tube light** works using **electric discharge through mercury vapor** in the presence of an **inert gas (usually argon)**.

**Main components inside a fluorescent tube:**

1. **Mercury vapor**
2. **Argon gas**
3. **Phosphor coating on the inside of the tube**

---

**How a Fluorescent Tube Works**

**Step-by-step process:**

1. When **electricity passes through the tube**, it excites **argon gas and mercury vapor**.

2. Excited **mercury atoms emit ultraviolet (UV) radiation**.

3. The **phosphor coating** on the tube wall **absorbs this UV light**.

4. The **phosphor converts UV radiation into visible white light**.

So the **main working substances inside the tube** are:

**Mercury vapor + Argon gas**

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Sodium Oxide and Argon**

**Sodium oxide** is a **solid compound**, not used in fluorescent lamps.

Fluorescent tubes require **gas discharge**, not solid oxides.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**B. Sodium Vapour and Neon**

**Sodium vapor lamps** are used in **street lights**, not fluorescent tubes.

**Neon** is mainly used in **advertising sign lamps**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**C. Mercury Vapour and Argon** ✅

Fluorescent lamps contain:

• **Low-pressure mercury vapor**

• **Argon gas**

Mercury produces **ultraviolet radiation**, which the **phosphor coating converts to visible light**.

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

**D. Mercuric Oxide and Neon**

**Mercuric oxide** is a **chemical compound**, not used in fluorescent tubes.

**Neon gas** is used in **neon sign lamps**, not in standard fluorescent tubes.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Important Exam Comparison**

**Lamp Type — Substance Used — Light Color**

Fluorescent lamp — **Mercury vapour + Argon** — **White**

Sodium vapour lamp — **Sodium vapour** — **Yellow**

Neon lamp — **Neon gas** — **Red / Orange**

Mercury vapour lamp — **Mercury vapour** — **Bluish white**

---

**Typical Exam Question**

**Which gas produces ultraviolet radiation in fluorescent lamps?**

➡ **Answer: Mercury vapor**

---

✔ **Final Answer:**

**Mercury Vapour and Argon**

questions.text: Which one of the following metals is accessed in the native state?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Gold**

Gold occurs naturally in **pure metallic form** because it is chemically less reactive.

questions.text: Which one of the following is the correct sequence of the given substances in the decreasing order of their densities?
questions.solution:
**Question**

**Which one of the following is the correct sequence of the given substances in the decreasing order of their densities?**

A. Steel > Mercury > Gold
B. Gold > Mercury > Steel
C. Steel > Gold > Mercury
D. Gold > Steel > Mercury

✅ **Correct Answer: B. Gold > Mercury > Steel**

---

**Core Concept**

**Density** is defined as:

$$ \text{Density} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}} $$

A substance with **higher density** means **more mass packed in a given volume**.

The question asks for **decreasing order of density**, meaning:

**Highest density → Lowest density**

---

**Densities of the Given Substances**

Approximate densities:

**Substance — Density (g/cm³)**

Gold — **19.3**

Mercury — **13.6**

Steel — **~7.8**

So the correct decreasing order is:

**Gold > Mercury > Steel**

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Steel > Mercury > Gold**

This option claims **steel is densest**, which is incorrect.

Actual densities:

Steel ≈ **7.8**

Mercury ≈ **13.6**

Gold ≈ **19.3**

Steel is actually the **least dense** among the three.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**B. Gold > Mercury > Steel** ✅

Gold has the **highest density**, followed by **mercury**, and **steel has the lowest density**.

**19.3 > 13.6 > 7.8**

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

**C. Steel > Gold > Mercury**

This option places **steel as most dense**, which is wrong.

Steel has **much lower density** than both **mercury and gold**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**D. Gold > Steel > Mercury**

Gold is correctly placed first, but **mercury is denser than steel**, so the order is wrong.

Correct comparison:

**Mercury > Steel**

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Important Exam Fact**

**Mercury is very dense**, which is why:

**Iron floats on mercury**

Even though **iron sinks in water**.

Example:

Density of iron ≈ **7.8 g/cm³**

Density of mercury ≈ **13.6 g/cm³**

---

**Quick Memory Trick**

Among common substances:

**Gold > Mercury > Iron/Steel > Water**

---

✔ **Final Answer:**

**Gold > Mercury > Steel**

questions.text: Of how many carats is the pure gold?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: 24 carat**

Carat indicates **purity of gold**.

Examples:
• 24 carat → Pure gold
• 22 carat → Jewelry gold

questions.text: The softest mineral, Talc (Soapstone) is mainly
questions.solution:
**Question**

**The softest mineral, Talc (Soapstone) is mainly**

A. Manganese Silicate
B. Sodium Silicate
C. Sodium Phosphate
D. Magnesium Silicate

✅ **Correct Answer: D. Magnesium Silicate**

---

**Core Concept**

**Talc** is the **softest mineral known** and is ranked **1 on the Mohs hardness scale**.

Chemical composition of talc:

$$ Mg_3Si_4O_{10}(OH)_2 $$

This formula shows that **talc is mainly a magnesium silicate mineral**.

Because of its **softness and smooth texture**, talc is often called **soapstone**.

---

**Why Talc Is Very Soft**

Talc has a **layered crystal structure**.

• Layers of **magnesium silicate sheets** are **weakly bonded**.

• These layers **slide easily over each other**.

Because of this structure, talc:

• Feels **very smooth**

• Can be **scratched easily**

---

**Uses of Talc**

Talc is used in:

• **Talcum powder**

• **Cosmetics**

• **Paper industry**

• **Ceramics**

• **Lubricants**

---

**Option-by-Option Explanation**

**A. Manganese Silicate**

Manganese silicates occur in **some rare minerals**, but they are **not talc**.

Talc **does not contain manganese** as the main element.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**B. Sodium Silicate**

**Sodium silicate** is also called **water glass**.

Uses include:

• **Adhesives**

• **Detergents**

• **Fireproofing materials**

It is **not related to talc**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**C. Sodium Phosphate**

**Sodium phosphate** is commonly used in:

• **Cleaning agents**

• **Food additives**

• **Water treatment**

It has **no relation to talc mineral composition**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**D. Magnesium Silicate** ✅

Talc is mainly composed of **magnesium silicate**.

This is why the correct chemical classification of talc is **hydrated magnesium silicate**.

✔ **Correct Answer**

---

📚 **Important Exam Fact**

**Mohs Hardness Scale**

Mineral — Hardness

Talc — **1 (softest)**

Gypsum — **2**

Calcite — **3**

Fluorite — **4**

Apatite — **5**

Feldspar — **6**

Quartz — **7**

Topaz — **8**

Corundum — **9**

Diamond — **10 (hardest)**

---

**Quick Memory Line (Very Useful)**

**Softest mineral → Talc**

**Hardest natural substance → Diamond**

---

✔ **Final Answer: Magnesium Silicate**

questions.solution:

Limestone consists mainly of **CaCO3**.

questions.solution:

• Layers of **magnesium silicate sheets** are **weakly bonded**.

questions.text: Mica is a
options: Good conductor of heat and bad conductor of electricity
Bad conductor of both heat and electricity
Good conductor of heat and electricity both
Bad conductor of heat and good conductor of electricity
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: A. Good conductor of heat and bad conductor of electricity**

This is a **classic General Science question** often asked in exams like **DRDO CEPTAM** because **Mica is a unique “rule-breaker” material** in terms of heat and electrical properties.

---

**Why Option A is Correct**

**Good Conductor of Heat**

Heat travels through **mica via vibrations in its crystal lattice** (called **phonons**).

Because of its **layered crystal structure**, these vibrations move efficiently, allowing **heat to pass through the material**.

Thus mica can **transfer heat effectively**.


**Bad Conductor of Electricity**

Mica is an **electrical insulator**.

Reasons:

• It **does not have free electrons** to carry electric current.

• It has **very high dielectric strength**, meaning it can **withstand high voltage without conducting electricity**.

Therefore mica **blocks electricity but allows heat transfer**.

---

**Explaining the Other Options**

**Option B — Bad conductor of heat and electricity**

❌ Incorrect

Many insulators such as **wood, plastic, and rubber** are poor conductors of both heat and electricity.

However, **mica is special** because it **conducts heat relatively well while still blocking electricity**.

---

**Option C — Good conductor of heat and electricity**

❌ Incorrect

Materials that conduct **both heat and electricity** are usually **metals**, such as:

• Copper
• Aluminium
• Silver

If mica conducted electricity, **electrical appliances like irons or heaters would become dangerous**.

---

**Option D — Bad conductor of heat and good conductor of electricity**

❌ Incorrect

This is the **exact opposite property of mica**.

Mica **does not conduct electricity** at all.

---

**Real-World Application (Important for Exams)**

**Example: Why is Mica used in an Electric Iron?**

Mica is used because:

• It **allows heat from the heating element to reach the metal plate**.

• It **blocks electricity from reaching the outer surface**.

Thus it **prevents electric shock while allowing efficient heating**.

---

📚 **Related Concepts to Remember**

**1. Thermal Conductivity**

The **ability of a material to transfer heat**.


**2. Dielectric Strength**

The **maximum electric field a material can withstand without becoming a conductor**.

Mica has **very high dielectric strength**.


**3. Graphite vs Diamond (Common Exam Comparison)**

Graphite

• **Good conductor of electricity**
• Soft layered structure

Diamond

• **Excellent conductor of heat**
• **Electrical insulator**

Mica behaves somewhat like **diamond in electrical insulation**, but still **conducts heat better than typical insulators**.

---

✔ **Final Answer:**

**Good conductor of heat and bad conductor of electricity**

questions.text: In which of the following industries is mica used as a raw material?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Electrical industry**.

Mica is used for:
• Electrical insulation
• Capacitors
• Electronic devices.

questions.text: Which one of the following liquids is very good conductor of heat?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: A. Mercury**

This question is a **typical General Science MCQ** asked in exams like **DRDO CEPTAM Tier-1**, focusing on **thermal conductivity of liquids**.

Among the given liquids, **Mercury is the best conductor of heat**.

---

**Why Mercury is the Correct Choice**

**1. Metallic Nature**

Mercury is the **only metal that remains liquid at room temperature**.

Because it is a **metal**, it contains a **large number of free electrons**.

These free electrons help in **rapid transfer of heat energy**.


**2. Heat Transfer Mechanism**

In metals, heat is transferred through:

• **Free electrons**

• **Atomic vibrations**

Because mercury has **mobile electrons**, heat can **travel faster through it compared to non-metallic liquids**.


**3. High Thermal Conductivity (Among Liquids Listed)**

Among the options such as **water, ether, and benzene**, mercury has the **highest thermal conductivity**.

This is why **mercury is widely used in thermometers**, as it **responds quickly to temperature changes**.

---

**Explaining the Other Options**

**Option B: Water**

Water is actually a **poor conductor of heat**.

Example experiment:

If you **heat water at the top of a test tube**, the **bottom remains cold for a long time**.

Reason:

Heat **does not travel efficiently through water by conduction**.

However, water transfers heat well through **convection** (movement of the liquid itself).

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**Option C: Ether**

Ether is an **organic compound**.

Organic liquids mainly have **covalent bonds** and **no free electrons**.

Therefore they **cannot transfer heat efficiently**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

**Option D: Benzene**

Benzene is also an **organic liquid**.

Like ether, it lacks **free electrons or ions** needed for efficient heat transfer.

Thus it behaves as a **thermal insulator compared to metals**.

❌ **Incorrect**

---

📚 **Related Concepts for DRDO / SSC Exams**

**1. Thermal Expansion**

Mercury **expands uniformly when heated**, which is another reason it is used in **thermometers**.


**2. Conduction vs Convection**

**Conduction**

Heat transfer through **direct molecular vibration or electron movement**.

Common in **solids and liquid metals**.


**Convection**

Heat transfer through **bulk movement of fluid molecules**.

Occurs in **liquids and gases**.


**3. Best Conductor of Heat (Solid)**

Among solids:

**Silver (Ag)** is the **best conductor of both heat and electricity**.


**4. Poor Conductors of Heat (Solids)**

Common thermal insulators:

• **Glass**

• **Wood**

• **Rubber**

---

✔ **Final Answer:**

**Mercury**

questions.text: Which one of the following metals is liquid at room temperature?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Mercury**.

Mercury is the only metal **liquid at room temperature (~25°C)**.

questions.text: Which one of the following metals is liquid at ordinary temperature?
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: Mercury**.

Again confirms mercury as **liquid metal under normal conditions**.

questions.text: Mercury is basically used in thermometer devices because its especiality is
questions.solution:
**Correct Answer: C. High circulation power (High Thermal Conductivity)**

**Explanation:** In many competitive exam translations, *high circulation power* refers to **high thermal conductivity**, meaning the ability to transfer heat quickly. **Mercury** is used in thermometers because it **conducts heat rapidly**, allowing the thermometer to respond quickly to temperature changes and give accurate readings.

**Option A: High Density** — Mercury is very dense (≈13.6 g/cm³), but density is the reason it is used in **barometers**, not thermometers.

**Option B: High Liquidity** — Mercury remains liquid over a wide range (−39°C to 357°C), but being liquid alone is not the main reason for its use in thermometers.

**Option C: High Circulation Power (Correct)** — Mercury conducts heat quickly because it is a **metal with free electrons**, allowing rapid temperature response in thermometers.

**Option D: High Specific Heat** — Incorrect. Mercury actually has **low specific heat**, meaning it requires little heat to change temperature, which helps it respond quickly.

**NCERT Reference:** Class 7 Science – Chapter 4 *Heat*, section *Measuring Temperature*. Mercury is used because it **expands uniformly, is shiny and visible inside glass, and does not stick to the glass tube**.

**Important Exam Facts:** Mercury **expands uniformly**, is **opaque and shiny**, and **does not stick to glass**, making it ideal for thermometers.

**Quick Concept Check:** Water is not used in thermometers because it **sticks to glass, is transparent (hard to see), and has very high specific heat so it reacts slowly to temperature changes**.
